Residence beyond three months requires meeting one of several conditions (Directive 2004/38/EC Art. 7): being a worker or self-employed, having sufficient resources plus comprehensive health insurance, being an enrolled student with resources and insurance, or being a qualifying family member of someone who meets one of those. This is an additional, better-secured right some readers can go on to claim -- it is never a condition on the entry/short-stay tier shown above, which is already unconditionally true for up to three months under Art. 6 ("without any conditions or any formalities other than the requirement to hold a valid identity card or passport"). A host state may ask for registration after arrival (Art. 8), but the Directive states plainly that holding a registration certificate "may under no circumstances be made a precondition for the exercise of a right" -- a formality, not a gate.

Since 2026-02-05, air-entry travelers of this nationality may use Mexico's SAE electronic pre-authorization (Sistema de Autorizacion Electronica) instead of a full consular visa -- single-entry, valid 30 days from issuance for use, stay length set case-by-case by the immigration officer at arrival. Air entry ONLY -- land/sea entry still requires a full visa for this nationality.

China/India nationals are NOT eligible for Argentina's AVE electronic authorization; they enter visa-exempt for 90 days if holding a valid US visa (B1/B2, B2, J, B1, O, P, E, or H-1B) or US green card. This base row is the true default absent that facility.

Indonesia has no diplomatic relations with Israel. Entry requires prior approval from the Directorate General of Immigration in Jakarta plus a multi-agency 'calling visa' review (immigration, Ministry of Foreign Affairs, intelligence/BAIS, police, military); permitted only via Soekarno-Hatta (Jakarta) or I Gusti Ngurah Rai (Bali) airports. Practically severe, not merely bureaucratic: only 51 calling visas were issued to Israeli nationals in the ~11 months through 2025-11-30 (immigration-intelligence data via CNBC Indonesia/Middle East Monitor) -- a near-total practical bar for ordinary tourism, though not a formal legal suspension. No religious-tourism or pilgrimage carve-out found.

China/India nationals are NOT eligible for Argentina's AVE electronic authorization; they enter visa-exempt for 90 days if holding a valid US visa (B1/B2, B2, J, B1, O, P, E, or H-1B) or US green card. This base row is the true default absent that facility.

This is an additional, better-secured right some readers can go on to claim -- it is never a condition on the entry/short-stay tier shown above, which is already unconditionally true for up to three months under Art. 6 ("without any conditions or any formalities other than the requirement to hold a valid identity card or passport").
